proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam description provides strong gameplay evidence: the game explicitly centers on turn-based battles, leveling up, gaining experience, growing bonds to unlock new abilities, traveling with a friend party, and using pair-specific Link Skills in combat. It also emphasizes an authored story about saving the world, explorable locations across two connected worlds, and environmental puzzles tied to world-switching. That combination strongly fits a story-forward JRPG structure with party progression and turn-based tactical encounters. Secondary CRPG/tactical signals exist from party management and puzzle-solving, but the strongest identity is JRPG-style story RPG rather than pure tactics or point-and-click adventure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-based turn-based RPG structure, companion ability synergy, authored adventure, and regular environmental puzzle solving between battles. Less aligned on modern-world mystery and bond-social progression specifics.

Strong fit on story-first indie RPG identity, turn-based party combat, emotional bonds, and dual-world exploration. Less clear on puzzle-solving density and mechanical party-link customization than the anchor.

Shares mystery-driven campaign, close friend group, relationship progression feeding combat, and movement between ordinary and alternate spaces. However, its calendar/social-sim dungeon loop is structurally more distinct than the anchor's exploration-puzzle JRPG flow.

Important comparison point for modern-setting, eccentric party JRPGs with linear exploration and turn-based progression. But available evidence here is thin, and it lacks the anchor's explicit puzzle/world-switching and bond-based combat emphasis.
Compact story RPG with a teen ensemble, turn-based party combat, relationship-driven character skills, and heavy overworld puzzle solving.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present an action-RPG loop centered on choosing one of four hero classes, fighting hordes of enemies and dragon bosses, acquiring skill loot, optimizing builds through the Ancestral Grid, evolving character and gear, and continuing into endgame activities. The game also emphasizes up-to-4-player co-op across the full adventure and a hub-city upgrade layer, which fits a co-op-capable loot-driven action RPG more than any match-based or shooter taxonomy result suggested by the noisy computed near-misses. Evidence is strong for dominant combat, class/build differentiation, gear/skill progression, and replayable campaign structure, but insufficient to lock perspective or exact world topology more precisely.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Class-based isometric action RPG with co-op play, large enemy packs, loot chasing, and endgame-focused build progression.
Four-player hack-and-slash RPG centered on distinct heroes, crowd-clearing combat, loot upgrades, and campaign-to-endgame co-op.
Fast-paced co-op dungeon-running with hordes of enemies, strong class identity, gear progression, and replayable endgame loops.
Action RPG with heavy build customization, skill specialization, loot optimization, and long-term character progression.
Accessible co-op loot ARPG with class-based combat, swarms of enemies, and continuous gear-and-skill build development.

The Mobile Infantry framing, battlefield combat, and anti-Bug arsenal support a military-FPS classification despite the sci-fi setting.

Classic-style first-person shooter focused on level exploration, key-hunting, secrets, and a solo campaign structure.
Arcade-style FPS centered on mowing down huge swarms with oversized weapons through straightforward combat-heavy missions.
Foundational retro FPS comp for speed, maze-like levels, secret discovery, and weapon-driven demon-slaying campaign flow.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Maestro as a VR orchestra conducting rhythm game. The core loop is timing- and gesture-driven: players use hand tracking to keep tempo, cue instruments, and shape music in real time, which strongly supports rhythm_game over weaker near-miss candidates. The game is described as playing through tracks/concert performances rather than exploration or narrative progression, fitting match_session structure, timing_input, rhythm_timing mechanics, and the hit_beats goal. It is single-player, non-combat, and presented as a premium replayable track-based experience with unlockable stages and cosmetics. While Steam also tags it as simulation/casual, that reads as secondary framing around realistic conducting technique rather than a different core genre.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Flagship VR rhythm comp: motion-controlled, song-based, precision-timing gameplay with strong replay and mastery overlap, though its slash-and-dodge loop is more arcade-reactive than conducting-focused.

Very solid VR rhythm neighbor built on full-arm movement, timing accuracy, and immersive musical flow; closer on expressive body performance, but still more note-lane driven than Maestro’s orchestral direction.

VR instrument-performance rhythm game with physical drumming motions, track progression, and mastery. Good overlap in musical embodiment, though its arcade campaign and demon-smashing framing push it away from Maestro’s refined conducting sim.

Another VR rhythm game with gesture-based strikes and track-by-track score chasing. Strong on tempo play and session structure, but the drumming/racing hybrid is less aligned with Maestro’s expressive hand-conducting identity.
VR music performance/creation with hand-led expressive control and stage presence, closer to conducting feel than target-hitting rhythm games.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an online shared-world sandbox adventure with third-person exploration, building, terrain destruction, co-op/MMO-style social play, and some monster fighting. However, the core gameplay identity does not cleanly match any allowed archetype. It is not well-supported as mmo_action_rpg because progression structure, RPG build identity, and combat loop are underspecified; not open_world_action_adventure because construction/sandbox settlement play appears equally or more central than authored action-adventure structure; and not exploration_survival_adventure because survival pressures are absent. The current mapping-gap signals also show no candidate archetype reached threshold. Because the evidence is concrete but points to a gameplay blend outside the available taxonomy rather than a strong fit within it, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a mismatched archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on colorful voxel presentation, online/shared-world play, building, exploration, destructibility, and light action combat; heavier class/loot focus than Altiros, but still the clearest benchmark.

Persistent online voxel sandbox with settlement building, world sculpting, exploration, and social progression maps well to Altiros's MMO-like sandbox identity; less explicitly action-combat-driven and more economy/city oriented.

Strong fit on third-person co-op sandbox adventuring, block building, island-based exploration, and real-time combat. It is more session-based and RPG-structured than Altiros's shared-world sandbox emphasis.

Good comp for building, terrain manipulation, exploration, creatures, and multiplayer sandbox play, but it leans more into survival/crafting systems and less into the cute action-adventure MMO social identity Altiros presents.
Shared-world block sandbox with freeform building, terrain destruction, exploration, and light combat as parallel pillars.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Pizza Slice as a restaurant business simulation centered on managing a family pizzeria: shopping for ingredients, prep work, making pizzas, expanding tables, buying better kitchenware, learning recipes, keeping customers happy, and growing from a small shop into a larger franchise. Metacritic explicitly calls it a strategy/simulation game with economic elements and a pizzeria manager role. Steam categories also support single-player, co-op, and PvP, with the detailed description naming an additive team-vs-team cook-off mode; however, the dominant identity remains the core business-management sandbox rather than competitive match play. The evidence strongly fits management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue and franchise growth, not urban planning or autonomous colony management.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct pizzeria economic sim with chain expansion, recipe creation, competition, and rise-from-small-shop progression; this is the clearest gameplay-identity match.
Very close on pizza-order fulfillment, hands-on making, upgrades, rival pressure, and keeping the shop afloat, though typically lighter in management scope.
Strong restaurant-ownership sandbox with menu design, layout, staff, recipes, and business growth; less specifically pizza-focused but highly aligned on core tycoon loop.
Hospitality tycoon with supply chain, staff management, customer satisfaction, recipe creation, and business expansion, but café management is a looser fit than restaurant/pizzeria sims.
Blends hands-on cooking with restaurant layout, upgrades, automation, and prominent co-op play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mythmatch as a narrative-rich town/community-building merge game focused on rebuilding Ithaca, matching items and people, relationships, and replayable god-themed minigames. However, the evidence does not strongly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a city_builder, farming_sim, life_sim, management_tycoon, or puzzle-led archetype within the available list, and the referenced minigames appear additive rather than the core identity. Provider metadata also produces weak or noisy signals (for example co-op/multiplayer categories without concrete gameplay structure, and a simulation genre tag). Because the game’s core gameplay identity appears to sit outside the current archetype set, classification into an existing archetype would be speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong overlap in cozy narrative progression, helping a cast of characters, and building/improving a shared home space, but Spiritfarer is fundamentally a management/exploration/crafting game rather than a merge-driven town builder.

The horror framing is present, but there is not enough evidence for a supported core archetype such as survival_horror, psychological_horror, action_horror, or stealth_action_adventure: combat is not established, resource-survival structure is absent, puzzle structure is unclear, and stealth is only implied by hide-and-seek/chase wording. The available text points more strongly to a short first-person narrative/walking-sim experience, which is not an available taxonomy archetype here, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

The text also indicates freer roaming across Neokos rather than purely linear mission flow, which fits a semi-open campaign CRPG better than a pure tactics game. Turn-based tactics is a strong secondary identity, but the party creation, roleplay framing, questing, and choice emphasis make party_based_crpg the better primary archetype.

Squad-based turn-based RPG emphasizing encounter tactics, party synergies, exploration, and flexible quest progression across a campaign.
Modern party CRPG with turn-based tactical combat, extensive character builds, companion management, and branching quest-driven progression.

The core identity is non-combat, low-pressure domestic/occupational simulation with customization and social-town flavor, which fits life_sim best. Repairing objects 'each with its own puzzle' adds a light hidden-object/puzzle-adjacent layer, while selling items and maintaining the store gives a management secondary aspect, but the evidence most strongly supports a cozy life-sim structure rather than a pure tycoon or puzzle game.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest match: cozy single-player restoration of sentimental household objects through hands-on repair puzzles, with townsfolk stories closely mirroring the anchor's core loop.

This strongly supports a precision_platformer classification with level-based, side-scrolling 2D, non-combat gameplay.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D precision-platforming, compact room challenges, puzzle-inflected traversal, and retry-heavy execution. Less aligned on structure because VVVVVV is more interconnected/open than the anchor’s explicitly level-based single-screen stages.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the core loop as a 'chaotic Roguelite Twin-Stick Shooter with a Base Defense Twist' playable with up to 4 players. The text describes repeated runs with time reversal, retaining experience/resources between attempts, which strongly supports roguelite run structure plus metaprogression. Moment-to-moment play centers on exploring a planet, gathering resources, fighting monsters in arena encounters and waves, then returning to defend the crashed ship using crafted ramparts, turrets, and traps. Combat is dominant, fast, and top-down/twin-stick in identity, with multiple class/loadout styles spanning melee, explosives, flamethrowers, turrets, missiles, dashes, and ranged attacks. Co-op is explicitly emphasized in Steam categories and main copy, making co_op_action_roguelite the best fit. The base-defense/crafting layer is meaningful but additive rather than enough to displace the action-roguelite identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on run-based drafting, arcade-machine feel, kinetic board interactions, and absurd combo engines. Pachinko scoring differs from claw grabbing, but the physics-plus-synergy identity is highly compatible.

Excellent match on roguelike deckbuilder structure, randomized drafting, and payoff-combo construction inside a gambling-machine frame. Less tactile/physics-driven than Cupiclaw, but very aligned in buildcraft and run logic.

Shares physics-driven aiming, run progression, relic/orb synergies, and arcade roots. However, Peglin is fundamentally turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw's machine-management combo loop.
A run-based combo engine builder focused on drafting synergies and exploiting random systems for escalating score output.
Uses run-based drafting and spatial board optimization to create item combos, with progression driven by build shaping more than reflex combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor in the same series with nearly identical point-and-click horror-adventure structure, grotesque cartoon style, linear progression, and puzzle-led play.
Very close match on single-player horror point-and-click gameplay, cursor-driven puzzle solving, and story-led progression, though it leans more psychological and narrative-heavy.
Strong fit as a dark 2D point-and-click puzzle adventure with unsettling imagery, but its non-linear action-reaction design is less aligned with the anchor’s more authored linear flow.
Good interface and puzzle-investigation overlap, but the comedic episodic micro-case structure is a noticeable step away from the anchor’s grotesque linear horror adventure.
Dark point-and-click puzzle adventure with linear progression, creepy storybook horror, and puzzle-gated exploration.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present the game as a shop/store management sandbox first: designing the shop layout, organizing shelves, setting prices, managing staff, crafting stock and upgrades, and building barricades/traps. That strongly fits management_tycoon with systemic sandbox/base growth. The zombie defense layer is significant but appears additive to the management loop rather than the main identity; combat is described as defending the shop during raids and hordes, not as the core session structure. Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong overlap in recruiting/equipping survivors, base development, scavenging, and zombie-driven defense decisions. Weaker on the anchor's store-management, pricing, and retail optimization loop, so it is adjacent rather than truly same.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D co-op platforming, coordinated traversal, hazard navigation, and level-based structure; more puzzle-specialized and more explicitly co-op-dependent than Soulshard.
Strong fit on side-scrolling traversal, environmental obstacles, local co-op plus solo play, and low-combat challenge structure; less minimalist and more cinematic than the anchor.
Good match for trap-heavy stage challenges, precise movement, solo/couch co-op support, and platform-focused difficulty; somewhat more twitchy and puzzle-stunt driven.

Fits teamwork platforming, obstacle-course progression, and environmental interaction well, but is more gimmick-led and more strongly built around two-character cooperation than Soulshard seems to be.
2D puzzle-platformer with solo or local co-op, light/no combat focus, and progression built around hazard navigation and environmental cooperation.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit here: turn-based expedition structure, survival pressure, resource management, procedural hazards, and roguelike run tension align well, though it is more party/event driven and less puzzle-centric than Aquamarine.

Shares journey-based survival, supply management, hazard mitigation, and authored-progress campaign framing. It is less about environmental puzzle solving and alien-world exploration, but the survival-travel loop is meaningfully adjacent.

Very strong overlap in alien-ocean survival, scavenging, crafting upgrades, studying wildlife, and trying to get home. It falls short on gameplay identity because Subnautica is real-time, open-world, and more action-driven than Aquamarine's turn-based, puzzle-forward structure.
Exploration-led sci-fi mystery with low combat, deliberate navigation, and puzzle-like discovery, though it is more narrative-linguistic than survival-focused.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions repeatedly frame the game as 'survival horror' and add concrete loop details: combat, investigations, puzzles, and resource management. The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A very close match on core loop: tense exploration, limited resources, puzzle solving, authored campaigns, and survival-horror combat pressure in a modern Resident Evil format.

Strong modern RE survival-horror comp with inventory pressure, linear campaign progression, and combat/horror balance, though it leans more action-forward than the anchor’s classic investigative framing.
Good third-person survival-horror neighbor with combat, stealth, limited ammo, exploration, and narrative progression, but its semi-open structure and stealth/trap emphasis make it a looser fit.
Relevant especially for Requiem’s Leon/action-horror side, but RE4’s set-piece pacing and heavier combat emphasis sit somewhat farther from classic puzzle-gated survival-horror identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on city-building fundamentals: multiple settlements, hostile weather, needs balancing, production chains, and persistent network-level progression. The main mismatch is its roguelite run structure versus Laysara's steadier sandbox/campaign buildout.

Excellent fit on economy design, citizen-need ladders, inter-settlement trade, and logistics optimization. It is less survival-focused and includes diplomacy/warfare and broader empire play, but the production-network identity is very relevant.

Strong overlap in terrain-aware building, infrastructure routing, environmental hazard preparation, and sandbox settlement optimization. It is more colony-survival and water/terraforming-driven than Laysara's multi-town mountain trade focus.

A solid combat-free settlement survival builder with resource planning, population management, and town sustainability. It is a simpler, more traditional single-settlement survival sim with less emphasis on transport engineering and interconnected settlements.
Deep logistics-first city building with intricate transport networks, production chains, and infrastructure optimization over combat.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: arcade-origin 2D action-platforming, stage clears, projectile combat, hazards, and boss encounters align strongly with the anchor’s core loop.

The premise centers on character interaction between the demon queen and princess, supporting an authored linear interpersonal drama structure within a fantasy setting.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit on top-down exploration, hidden routes, minimal handholding, secrets, and light action-adventure structure. More puzzle/manual-decoding and tougher combat than Afterplace, but the discovery loop is highly aligned.

Good match for retro-styled top-down roaming, branching paths, secrets, and action combat. It is notably harsher, faster, and lighter on dialogue/choice structure than Afterplace, making it a slightly less direct fit.

Overlaps on top-down melee combat, secrets, exploration, and light stat/ability progression. However, it is more structured, boss-driven, and punishment-focused than Afterplace’s freer open-world wandering.
Dreamlike top-down exploration with loose structure, hidden areas, odd characters, and discovery-first progression over hard combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D co-op brawler/RPG hybrid built around classes, loot, repeated runs, and combo-heavy side-scrolling combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 3D hack-and-slash action RPG centered on fast-paced combat, a 20+ character roster, and 50+ story missions. The Steam detailed description gives the strongest identity signals: '3D HACK-AND-SLASH REBORN,' 'mix and match flashy combat techniques,' 'evolvable abilities,' and 'create an unstoppable team' all support a character-action core with party-management elements rather than a pure solo combo-action game. The presence of a primary story mode following Mask of Truth, a base system for interacting with companions and strengthening characters, and an online/single-player Dream Arena side mode supports a campaign-driven, mission-based structure with secondary JRPG-story-RPG traits. Evidence is strong enough to accept, but not strong enough to place it in a more specific action-RPG subtype from the allowed list.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same core mission-based anime hack-and-slash structure, playable roster, and story adaptation format.

Mission-structured action game with a large cast, relationship/base interactions, unit development, and tactical party management elements.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Pogui as a side-scrolling platformer with hazardous levels, running/jumping/dashing, stamina-managed movement, and world-by-world stage progression. Metacritic explicitly calls it a 'side-scrolling precision platformer' in retro pixel art style, which strongly supports precision_platformer as the core identity. The game appears level-based across 5 themed worlds with handcrafted challenges and traversal-focused play. Enemy presence and 'defeat this nightmare' language indicate light action elements, making action_platformer a reasonable secondary archetype, but the dominant loop is precise movement and obstacle navigation rather than combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Tight side-scrolling precision platforming built around dash-based movement, hazard-heavy stages, and short challenge-focused level design.
Fast, exacting 2D platforming with quick retries, compact obstacle courses, and a strong emphasis on movement mastery over exploration.
Level-based 2D platformer with themed worlds, fluid movement, enemy-filled stages, and frequent mechanic twists from one level set to the next.
Cute but demanding side-scrolling platformer with world-by-world progression, precise jumps, enemies, and a polished retro-inspired feel.
Another compact, difficult 2D precision platformer focused on dangerous stages, exact timing, and survival through hostile environments.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. That strongly matches a run-based deck-construction game with buildcraft, metaprogression/unlocks, tactical optimization, and boss encounters. Although the theme is dice rather than literal cards, the core gameplay identity is clearly deckbuilder/card-battler adjacent, and card_battler is the closest supported archetype in the taxonomy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Explicit dice-deck-building roguelike with run-based drafting, dice-pool construction, tactical turns, and heavy synergy optimization; closest gameplay match in the set.
Turn-based deckbuilding roguelike centered on manipulating dice to power drafted spells across repeated encounters; very close loop despite a more combat-spell framing.

Run-based dice roguelike with class-specific builds, equipment synergies, and tactical combat. Slightly less direct because it is more equipment/combat-encounter driven than deckbuilder-score engine driven.
Strong overlap in luck-pushing, debt-clearing run structure, build synergies, and break-the-economy scoring, but the slot-machine symbol engine is more adjacent than a true dice/deck combat builder.
Roguelike deckbuilder where dice replace cards, with run-based drafting, synergies, and tactical turn-by-turn optimization.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a Reigns-style swipe-driven narrative/choice game: 'swipe right, swipe left,' 'thousands of storyline combinations,' 'navigate the moral maze,' puzzle-solving, unlocking deeds/characters, and leveling up Dandelion's career. That is strong evidence for a branching choice-centric design, but the allowed archetype set does not include a clear Reigns/interactive-choice/narrative-card archetype. Available action RPG or beat 'em up candidates in the current taxonomy debug payload are contradicted by the actual text, which does not support third-person melee action as the core gameplay loop. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet does not map cleanly and safely to an allowed archetype, rejection is the most accurate outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The original template: left/right card swipes, balancing consequences, short runs, and branching narrative outcomes.
A direct follow-up built on the same swipe-choice structure, stat management, unlocks, and event-chain storytelling.
Another licensed Reigns variant using the same core loop of binary decisions, multiple rulers, and run-based branching stories.
Extends the same Reigns formula with swipes, narrative event chains, character encounters, and light strategic resource balancing.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only that this is a single-player action/adventure remake tied to the City Hunter license. The descriptions are almost entirely promotional and story-focused, with no concrete gameplay information about perspective, traversal, combat loop, mission structure, world topology, or progression. Because the gameplay identity is not described with enough specificity to distinguish among multiple action-adventure archetypes, the evidence is too thin for a reliable taxonomy mapping.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Anime-styled side-scrolling action with mission structure and character-driven single-player progression.
Modern revival of a classic action format with straightforward single-player stage-based brawling.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored description consistently identifies LOVE as a challenging retro platforming game built around 16 levels, speedrun mode, competitive scoring/leaderboards, and a unique checkpoint-respawn mechanic. That strongly supports precision_platformer over broader platformer archetypes: the core loop is stage clearing through difficult platforming challenges rather than combat, exploration, or collection. Evidence supports level-based progression, platform-navigation mechanics, score-attack replayability, mastery-heavy input demands, and no meaningful combat emphasis.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: High-difficulty level-based precision platforming with instant restarts, tight movement, and strong speedrun appeal.
Retro-styled minimalist platformer focused on harsh challenge, exact movement, and compact room-by-room obstacle design.
Pure mechanical platforming with minimal visuals, demanding stages, leaderboard competition, and an emphasis on mastery.
Precision platforming with dense challenge rooms, forgiving respawns, and a strong replay/speedrun loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is materially contradictory. Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently present Supernova as a space-themed Suika-style matching puzzle focused on combining planets for high score, while the OpenCritic description instead describes a sci-fi MOBA/strategy game with upgradeable armies, commanders, and crafting from drops. These imply fundamentally different gameplay identities, and neither line of evidence is strong enough to cleanly map into a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype with confidence. Because the bundle appears to conflate different titles or contains unreliable metadata, classification should be rejected.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct match for the presumed anchor identity: same drop-and-merge puzzle loop, same escalating object-combination structure, and similar score/space optimization.
Strong puzzle pedigree and shared falling-piece spatial pressure, but its line-clearing tetromino play is materially different from Suika-style physics merging.
Near-direct Suika-like comp built around dropping, bouncing, and merging fruit in a confined playfield for score.
Falling-piece score-attack puzzle that rewards chain planning and space control in a compact board.
Strong puzzle crossover comp for players who like score pressure, stacking decisions, and spatial optimization.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Royal Vermin as a 2D local platform fighter for 2-4 players, explicitly 'inspired by the best platform fighters.' The core loop is match-based PvP: players climb, throw, use ranged chargeable projectiles, knock opponents out of collapsing arenas, and aim to win rounds in fast, chaotic matches. Side-scrolling presentation, duel-focused combat, dominant combat presence, and win-match goals strongly support traditional_fighter over platformer; platforming is present but serves the fighting format rather than precision challenge progression. Party-game qualities are notable through local same-screen multiplayer and chaotic comeback-focused design, but they are secondary to the fighter identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest comp: multiplayer platform-fighter matches focused on knockbacks, ring-outs, stage hazards, and easy-to-learn party play.
Free-to-play platform fighting with accessible controls, side-view arenas, item-based weapon/projectile play, and chaotic multiplayer rounds.
A more mechanics-driven platform fighter sharing the same side-on arena combat, aerial movement, edge pressure, and knockout-based match flow.
Another accessible platform brawler centered on fast local-versus matches, stage control, launches, and party-friendly crossover chaos.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Ella Stars as an 'action-rhythm adventure' built around 30 original songs, with explicit rhythm gameplay, epic/intense boss battles, perfect combo finishers, adjustable difficulty, and single-player play. That strongly supports rhythm_game as the core identity, with timing-based play and boss-focused encounters as the main loop. Evidence for exact camera/topology is not explicit, so those fields are left empty rather than inferred.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit: action combat explicitly synced to music, authored stages, and stylish boss encounters align well with Ella Stars' action-rhythm and boss-focused loop, though Hi-Fi Rush is a larger 3D character-action game.

Music-driven action-adventure with colorful boss fights and combat built around musical energy is close in audience and structure, even if its combat is less strictly rhythm-timing gated than Ella Stars.

Strong song-by-song battle identity and memorable rhythm-centric encounters fit the boss-driven music angle well, but Everhood plays more like pattern-dodging musical battles than melee action-rhythm adventure.

Clear rhythm-game overlap with original tracks, hand-drawn presentation, single-player structure, and accessible difficulty options, but it lacks Ella Stars' combat-heavy and boss-centric action layer.
Rhythm-timed action platforming built around authored stages, original music, and repeated boss fights with a stylish, character-driven presentation.

title: Welcome to Doll Town

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a single-player, third-person horror experience centered on exploration, mystery-solving, eerie puzzles, and escape rather than combat. The cursed village, haunted dollhouses, environmental storytelling, and emphasis on dread and mystery support a narrative-driven psychological horror classification more strongly than action_horror or survival_horror, both of which lack concrete combat evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong match on third-person town exploration, puzzle solving, narrative-driven progression, and psychological mystery, even if it has more direct combat than the anchor likely does.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies SealChain as a single-player roguelike centered on item-linking, formations/seals, random events, character builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. However, the available text does not establish a strong fit to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a card battler despite combinatorial item synergies, not clearly turn-based tactics despite strategy language, and not supportable as co_op_action_roguelite because the evidence indicates single-player only. The Metacritic 'Top-Down Shoot-'Em-Up' label is too weak and isolated to anchor a shooter archetype. Overall the gameplay identity is real but falls between the available archetypes, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strongest blend of action roguelike combat and inventory-layout-driven progression. The spatial equipment management is a close mechanical neighbor to SealChain's item-linking and formation optimization.

Excellent match on run-based item placement, adjacency synergies, and build-defining inventory optimization. It lands slightly lower because its core combat loop is more turn-based/deckbuilding-oriented than SealChain's apparent action emphasis.
A solid action roguelike benchmark for randomized runs, monster combat, and extreme item-combo build mutation. Less precise because it lacks the anchor's spatial item-formation puzzle layer.

Fits top-down run-based combat, distinct characters, heavy item/stat stacking, and fast power scaling. Weaker than above options because its wave-survival structure and simpler equipment logic are farther from SealChain's formation/linking identity.
Roguelite progression centered on deliberate placement and combinatorial synergies, capturing the anchor’s formation optimization more than standard action roguelites do.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on dark fantasy action roguelike structure, punishing melee combat, dodge timing, trap-filled runs, and repeated procedural boss-focused attempts.
Essential benchmark for fast top-down action roguelike runs built on repeated deaths, enemy pattern mastery, bosses, and build variation, though it is more narrative and meta-progression heavy.
Strong fit on action-roguelite runs, skilled dodge-centric combat, bosses, and bleak atmosphere, but its waking-life/social progression makes the structure less purely combat-run driven.
Reasonable match through hack-and-slash roguelite runs, procedural dungeons, and boss combat, but it is more story-driven, character-RPG oriented, and partly co-op framed than the anchor.
Dark fantasy action roguelite with rapid melee combat, dodge-based survival, boss fights, and repeated procedural runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-based turn-based JRPG structure, continent exploration, dungeon travel, political conflict, and gear/crafting progression. Less distinctive on town trust/supply systems, but the core play identity is highly aligned.

Excellent fit on classic overworld-to-dungeon JRPG flow, turn-based party combat, rebellion/war themes, recruitable allies, and headquarters/town progression. Slightly broader and more ensemble-driven, but very compatible for audience expectation.

Strong story-driven JRPG comp with party management, turn-based battles, and anti-regime campaign momentum. It appears more linear and less exploration-and-systems driven than the anchor, so the fit is good but not top tier.

Shares traditional turn-based JRPG combat, party progression, town interaction, and open destination choice. However, its eight-protagonist structure and chapter-based progression are a looser match for the anchor’s party campaign and town-supply progression loop.
Isometric party RPG with kingdom restoration, town progression, character bonding, and tactical turn-based battles.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Ys X: Proud Nordics as the definitive edition of an action RPG adventure centered on fast-paced combat, exploration of a large island/map area, boss fights, and an authored story campaign. The copy emphasizes 'fast-paced combat,' 'Cross Action,' 'Mana Action,' new bosses, a high-difficulty dungeon, and broad map/exploration content, all of which support a single-player fantasy action-RPG identity. The current computed taxonomy pointing to traditional_fighter is contradicted by the actual descriptive text, which clearly frames a narrative-driven exploratory action RPG rather than a PvP or match-based fighter. The strongest fit in the available taxonomy is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, though the world structure reads more semi-open than fully open world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Proud Nordics is the enhanced definitive edition of the original, retaining the same core combat, exploration, progression, and story foundation.

Falcom action RPG with quick combo-based combat, character swapping, dungeon runs, and progression built around combat plus narrative segments.
A recognizable action RPG comp with flashy real-time party combat, special team attacks, boss fights, and linear-to-open area exploration.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Nearly identical core loop: drop items into a container, merge matching pieces into larger ones, prevent overflow, and chase high scores in short replayable runs.
Strong on falling-piece spatial management, topping-out pressure, and replayable score-focused puzzle flow, but line-clearing Tetris rules are materially different from merge-evolution play.
Direct Suika-like: drop fruit into a container, use physics positioning, merge matching pieces, and chase higher scores before topping out.
Clean high-score merge puzzle benchmark centered on combining like pieces into higher values, but with swipe movement instead of falling physics.
Shares arcade puzzle pressure, stack management, and quick-session replayability, though its match/chaining rules are less like-for-like than Suika-style merging.
